body_text:
Section 5.3 Improper Integrals Involving Rational and Exponential Functions
Solution Step 1: Show that the integral converges. The integrand is continu so it is enough to show that it has a convergent integral on $[1, \infty)$. We 1 $\frac{1}{x^{\alpha}+1} \leq \frac{1}{x^{\alpha}}$, where the latter has a convergent integral over $[1, \infty)$.
Step 2: Apply the substitution $x=e^{t}$. Let $x=e^{t}, d x=e^{t} d t$, and note that varies from 0 to $\infty, t$ varies from $-\infty$ to $\infty$, and so
$$
I=\int_{0}^{\infty} \frac{1}{x^{\alpha}+1} d x=\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \frac{e^{t}}{e^{\alpha t}+1} d t=\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \frac{e^{x}}{e^{\alpha x}+1} d x
$$
where, for convenience, in the last integral we have used $x$ as a variable of integrat instead of $t$. As we change $x$ to $z$, we obtain the function $f(z)=\frac{e^{z}}{e^{\alpha z}+1}$ with p at the roots of $e^{\alpha z}+1=0$. Since the exponential function is $2 \pi i$-periodic, ther
$$
e^{\alpha z}=-1=e^{i \pi} \Leftrightarrow \alpha z=i \pi+2 k \pi i \quad \Leftrightarrow \quad z_{k}=(2 k+1) \frac{\pi}{\alpha} i, k=0, \pm 1, \pm 2, .
$$

Thus $f(z)=\frac{e^{z}}{e^{\alpha z}+1}$ has infinitely many poles at $z_{k}$, all lying on the imaginary a (Figure 6).
Step 3: Selecting the contour of integration. Our contour should expand in $x$-direction in order to cover the entire $x$-axis. But since $f(z)$ has infinitely ma poles on the $y$-axis, to avoid dealing with an infinite sum of residues, we will not an expanding semicircle in the upper half-plane. Instead, we will use a rectangu contour $\gamma_{R}$ consisting of the paths $\gamma_{1}, \gamma_{2}, \gamma_{3}$, and $\gamma_{4}$, as in Figure 6, and let denote the path integral of $f(z)$ over $\gamma_{j}(j=1, \ldots, 4)$. As $R \rightarrow \infty, \gamma_{R}$ will expa in the horizontal direction, but the length of the vertical sides remains fixed at To understand the reason for our choice of the vertical length, let us consider and $I_{3}$. On $\gamma_{1}$, we have $z=x, d z=d x$,
$$
I_{1}=\int_{-R}^{R} \frac{e^{x}}{e^{\alpha x}+1} d x
$$
and so $I_{1}$ converges to the desired integral $I$ as $R \rightarrow \infty$. On $\gamma_{3}$, we have $z=x+i d z=d x$, and using the $2 \pi i$-periodicity of the exponential function, we get
$$
I_{3}=\int_{R}^{-R} \frac{e^{x+i \frac{2 \pi}{\alpha}}}{e^{\alpha\left(x+i \frac{2 \pi}{\alpha}\right)}+1} d x=-e^{\frac{2 \pi}{\alpha} i} \int_{-R}^{R} \frac{e^{x}}{e^{\alpha x}+1} d x=-e^{\frac{2 \pi}{\alpha} i} I_{1}
$$

This last equality explains the choice of the vertical sides: They are chosen so th the integral on the returning horizontal side $\gamma_{3}$ is equal to a constant multiple the integral on $\gamma_{1}$. From here the solution is straightforward.
Step 4: Applying the residue theorem. From Step 2, we have only one pole of $f$ inside $\gamma_{R}$ at $z_{0}=\frac{\pi}{\alpha} i$. Since $e^{\alpha z}+1$ has a simple root, this is a simple pole. Usi Proposition 1(ii), Section 5.1, we find
$$
\operatorname{Res}\left(\frac{e^{z}}{e^{\alpha z}+1}, \frac{\pi}{\alpha} i\right)=\frac{e^{\frac{\pi}{\alpha} i}}{\alpha e^{\alpha \frac{\pi}{\alpha} i}}=\frac{e^{\frac{\pi}{\alpha} i}}{\alpha e^{i \pi}}=-\frac{e^{\frac{\pi}{\alpha} i}}{\alpha}
$$
and so by the residue theorem
$$
I_{1}+I_{2}+I_{3}+I_{4}=\int_{\gamma_{R}} \frac{e^{z}}{e^{\alpha z}+1} d z=2 \pi i \operatorname{Res}\left(\frac{e^{z}}{e^{\alpha z}+1}, \frac{\pi}{\alpha} i\right)=-2 \pi i \frac{e^{\frac{\pi}{\alpha} i}}{\alpha}
$$
